title: How One Mom's 'Silly' Trivia Business Became a Community Empire
guestBio: Christina Wooldridge is the founder of Trivia Goat, a live entertainment company bringing tech-enabled trivia to bars, breweries, and restaurants. With a background in hotel/restaurant management and nonprofit fundraising, she's a working mom of two based in Orange County, CA who transformed her hospitality expertise into a thriving community-building business.
excerpt: What if your 'silly' business idea could outperform the serious ones? Christina Wooldridge turned a simple trivia concept into a thriving community empire while navigating motherhood and redefining what success really means.
metaDescription: Discover how Christina Wooldridge built Trivia Goat into a community-centered business while being a working mom. Hospitality meets entrepreneurship.
keyTakeaways: Hospitality isn't a soft skill—it's a leadership superpower that creates real power in any conversation.
Your business idea doesn't need to be prestigious to be valuable and profitable.
Human connection, not just products or services, is what keeps customers coming back long-term.
Scaling doesn't always mean doing more—sometimes it means packaging your expertise differently.
Motherhood sharpens leadership skills through forced intentionality and laser focus.
The real value in business comes from creating belonging and community, not just transactions.
Unconventional ideas often succeed because they solve real human needs that others overlook.

pullQuotes.text: Hospitality is not a soft skill — it's a leadership superpower
Your business idea doesn't have to be 'prestigious' to be valuable
The real thing that keeps customers coming back is human connection

timestamps.label: Christina's through line — from childhood parades to live events
Motherhood, identity, and finding value beyond productivity
What Trivia Goat is and how it works

content:
What if the business idea you've been dismissing as "silly" or "not serious enough" could actually become your most successful venture? That's exactly what happened to Christina Wooldridge, founder of Trivia Goat, who turned what seemed like a simple concept into a thriving community-building empire.

In this powerful conversation with Aimee Rickabus on The Manage Her Podcast, Christina shares how she transformed her hospitality background into a tech-enabled trivia business that's creating real connection in an increasingly disconnected world. But this isn't just a business success story—it's a masterclass in redefining value, embracing unconventional ideas, and building something meaningful while navigating the complexities of working motherhood.

The Thread That Connects Everything

Christina's entrepreneurial journey didn't start with a business plan—it started with childhood parades. As a young girl organizing neighborhood festivities, she was unknowingly developing the skills that would later become her superpower: bringing people together and creating experiences that matter. This thread of community-building wove through her hotel and restaurant management career, into nonprofit fundraising, and ultimately into founding Trivia Goat.

What's fascinating about Christina's path is how she recognized this pattern in her own life. Too often, we dismiss our natural inclinations as "not real work" or "just something we're good at." Christina shows us that these innate abilities—especially when they involve connecting people—can become the foundation of a profitable, meaningful business.

Motherhood as Leadership Training

One of the most striking insights from this conversation is how Christina views motherhood as leadership training rather than a career obstacle. When she became a mother, the usual quick fixes and band-aid solutions stopped working. Motherhood forced her to think long-term, to be intentional with her time, and to focus on what truly matters.

This shift in perspective transformed not just her parenting but her entire approach to business. The scattered, people-pleasing version of herself had to evolve into someone who could make clear decisions, set boundaries, and operate with laser focus. As Christina puts it, motherhood didn't diminish her professional capabilities—it sharpened them.

The revelation that her worth wasn't tied to her productivity was game-changing. In a culture that equates busy with important, Christina learned to separate her identity from her output. This mindset shift freed her to pursue ideas that genuinely excited her rather than those that simply looked impressive on paper.

Hospitality as Your Secret Weapon

If you've ever worked in hospitality, you might not realize you're sitting on a goldmine of transferable skills. Christina makes a compelling case that hospitality isn't a "soft skill"—it's a leadership superpower that creates real power in any conversation or situation.

Think about what hospitality really means: reading people's needs before they voice them, making others feel comfortable and valued, managing multiple priorities seamlessly, and turning potentially negative experiences into positive ones. These aren't nice-to-have abilities—they're essential leadership competencies that translate directly into business success.

Christina discovered that her hospitality background gave her a unique advantage in difficult conversations and challenging situations. The ability to create connection and find common ground isn't just useful for managing restaurant customers—it's invaluable for negotiating deals, managing team conflicts, and building lasting business relationships.

The Human Connection Advantage

In an increasingly digital world, Christina identified a crucial gap: genuine human connection. Trivia Goat isn't just about asking questions and keeping score—it's about creating spaces where people feel they belong. The technology enables the experience, but the human element drives the value.

What many venue owners misunderstand, according to Christina, is what actually keeps customers coming back. It's not just good food, competitive prices, or convenient location—though those matter. It's the feeling that they're part of something, that they're known and welcomed, that their presence matters to the community.

This insight extends far beyond the hospitality industry. In whatever business you're building, the companies that thrive long-term are those that prioritize human connection alongside their core offering. People don't just buy products or services—they buy belonging, recognition, and the feeling that they matter.

Scaling Without Sacrificing Presence

One of the biggest challenges for any service-based business is scaling without losing the personal touch that made it special in the first place. Christina solved this through what she calls a "self-serve model"—not in the traditional sense, but by packaging her expertise and systems in a way that maintains quality while allowing growth.

The key was recognizing that she didn't need to be physically present at every trivia night to ensure success. By developing robust training systems, clear processes, and empowering her team members, she created a business that could grow without requiring her constant oversight. This approach allowed her to maintain her presence as a mother while building a scalable enterprise.

This is a crucial lesson for any entrepreneur, especially working mothers: scaling doesn't always mean doing more yourself. Sometimes it means stepping back and creating systems that allow others to deliver your vision consistently.

The Moment Everything Clicked

There's a powerful moment Christina describes when she realized she wasn't just running trivia nights—she was creating belonging. Watching strangers become friends, seeing regular customers who planned their weeks around trivia night, witnessing the genuine community that formed around what started as a "silly" business idea—this is when she understood the real value of her work.

This realization is crucial for any entrepreneur questioning whether their idea is "serious enough" or "important enough." Value isn't determined by complexity or prestige—it's determined by how well you solve real human needs. Christina's business succeeds because it addresses a fundamental human need for connection and community in an accessible, enjoyable way.

Permission to Pursue the Unconventional

Perhaps the most important message from Christina's story is the permission to explore unconventional ideas. In a world that often values traditional markers of success, it takes courage to pursue something that others might dismiss as frivolous or unimportant.

Christina's advice is clear: give yourself permission to explore what genuinely interests you, even if it doesn't fit conventional definitions of a "serious business." The market will tell you whether your idea has value—but only if you're brave enough to test it.

Your unconventional idea might just outperform the serious ones because it solves real problems in ways that others overlook. Sometimes the best businesses come from following your natural inclinations and trusting that what brings you joy might also bring value to others.

Christina Wooldridge's journey from hospitality professional to community-building entrepreneur proves that success doesn't require choosing between meaningful work and profitable business. By embracing her natural abilities, leveraging her unique perspective as a working mother, and having the courage to pursue an unconventional idea, she built something that creates both financial success and genuine human connection.
